title: The Toledo translation movement
blurb: In reconquered Spain, translators like Gerard of Cremona rendered Arabic science — astrology, alchemy, the Picatrix's sources — into Latin, flooding Europe with a magical learning it had lost.
mtg: The pipeline that carried the whole vocabulary of magic into Latin, and thence English.
source: Charles Burnett (renaissance magic corpus); Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy

title: Hugh of St Victor classifies the magic arts
blurb: Hugh's Didascalicon and its successors sorted the forbidden arts — mantic, maleficent, illusory — giving scholastic Europe a map of what counted as magic.
source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

title: The great bestiaries
blurb: Illuminated bestiaries like the Aberdeen Bestiary made each animal a moral allegory — the pelican as Christ, the panther's sweet breath as grace. Natural history as sermon.
mtg: When zoology was theology; the ancestor of the fantasy monster manual.

title: Michael Scot, court astrologer to Frederick II
blurb: Scot translated Aristotle and Arabic astrology and wrote on the occult sciences at the Sicilian court, becoming a legend — Dante puts him in Hell among the diviners.
source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

title: The Speculum astronomiae draws the line
blurb: Attributed to Albertus Magnus, this work tried to separate licit astronomical images from illicit necromantic ones — the anxious boundary-work at the heart of learned magic.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB context; Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic

title: The Picatrix enters Latin
blurb: At the court of Alfonso X of Castile the Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm was translated, and as the Picatrix it became the West's master-text of astral talisman-magic for four centuries.

title: Roger Bacon on the elixir and long life
blurb: In the Opus Majus and his alchemical works Bacon argued that a perfected elixir could prolong life and heal the body — alchemy as medicine and eschatology.
mtg: The elixir of life, argued in a Franciscan's Latin.
source: Meagan Allen, Roger Bacon and the Incorruptible Human (alchemy corpus)

title: The Ars Notoria — magic to learn faster
blurb: A ritual of prayers and visual figures promising the divine infusion of knowledge (scientia) — the medieval student's dream of downloading the curriculum by rite.
mtg: A grimoire that promised instant knowledge — pure MTG card-flavor, six centuries early.
source: Skinner & Clark (eds.), Ars Notoria; Fanger, Invoking Angels

title: Aquinas on demons and the pact
blurb: Thomas Aquinas argued that all magic beyond the natural implied a pact with demons — the theological reasoning that turned learned conjuring into heresy.
source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

title: The Sworn Book of Honorius (Liber Iuratus)
blurb: One of the oldest surviving grimoires, its rites for the beatific vision and the binding of spirits pass by oath (iuratus) from master to sworn disciple.
source: Joseph Peterson (ed.), The Sworn Book of Honorius; Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic

title: Pope John XXII moves against necromancy
blurb: Fearing magical attempts on his life, John XXII authorised inquisition into 'those who make a pact with hell,' hardening the Church's war on ritual magic.
source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages; Bailey, The Sacred and the Sinister

title: John of Morigny rewrites forbidden magic as devotion
blurb: A French monk, burned once by the Ars Notoria, wrote his own Liber Visionum — reframing knowledge-magic as visions granted by the Virgin. Claire Fanger's central case for how magic disguised itself as piety.

title: John of Rupescissa and the quintessence
blurb: A Franciscan visionary distilled alcohol and called it the quinta essentia — a heavenly incorruptible drawn out of matter to fortify the body against the coming apocalypse.
source: Leah DeVun, Prophecy, Alchemy, and the End of Time (alchemy corpus)

title: The necromancer's manual (Munich Handbook tradition)
blurb: Handbooks of image-magic and demon-conjuring circulated in a clerical underworld — the 'necromancy' that was really the illicit half of learned magic, copied and hidden by educated men.
mtg: The literal spellbooks the game's flavor imagines.
source: Kieckhefer, Forbidden Rites; Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic

title: Chaucer's Canon's Yeoman's Tale
blurb: Chaucer's satire of a swindling alchemist is also a precise account of the craft's apparatus and jargon — proof of how deep alchemy had sunk into English culture.

title: Eymerich's Directorium Inquisitorum
blurb: Nicholas Eymerich's inquisitor's manual argued that invoking demons was inherently heretical, supplying the legal machinery that would later drive the witch trials.
source: Bailey, The Sacred and the Sinister

title: England bans multiplying gold
blurb: A statute of Henry IV forbade the 'multiplication' of gold and silver by alchemy — a law that, by its very existence, shows how seriously transmutation was taken.
